verdict_summary: All 6 discriminative claims verified at file:line. Plan is coherent, Option A narrowing is non-regressive on the test suite, FP elimination quantification is sourced from in-repo M3-binary-smoke evidence (not speculation), and the residual gaps are correctly identified and documented as carry-forwards. M2 may proceed under kraken with the stated 10-entry allowlist. R1 (custom-named clients) and R2 (composed-access `self.client.get`) are correctly classified as TIGER-MEDIUM/LOW with explicit acceptance — these are not blockers, they are documented carry-forwards.

claims_verified.claim: Wildcards exist at taint.rs:2476-2477 inside RUST_AST_SINKS HttpRequest entry
claims_verified.evidence: Line 2476: `("*", "get"),`; Line 2477: `("*", "post"),`. AstSinkPattern struct at L2473-2488 with sink_type TaintSinkType::HttpRequest. Confirmed: 2 wildcard entries + 8 scoped-identifier raw-fallback entries; wildcard_count=2 matches investigation.json.

claims_verified.claim: test_e2e_rust_ssrf_reqwest_get at vuln.rs:1184 uses scoped-identifier `reqwest::get(target)`, NOT wildcard member-access

claims_verified.evidence: File contents (5 lines): `let u = std::env::var("U").unwrap(); reqwest::blocking::get(&u).unwrap();`. Matched via `("", "reqwest::blocking::get")` entry at taint.rs:2480 (raw-fallback, pat_rcv empty). Wildcard removal does NOT affect rust_ssrf_positive (vuln_migration_v1_red.rs:1670).

claims_verified.claim: ssrf_string_literal_fp.rs has no env_var source so wildcard match is irrelevant
claims_verified.evidence: Fixture is documentation strings only. `std::env::var`, `reqwest::blocking::get` appear ONLY inside string literals, never as call expressions. No source-to-sink taint flow possible regardless of wildcard.

claims_verified.claim: member_patterns_match treats `*` as receiver-NAME-text wildcard, not type-aware
claims_verified.evidence: L3871-3877: `if *pat_rcv == "*" { if field == *pat_field { return true; } }`. The match is purely on field-text equality when receiver is `*`; receiver text is the variable identifier name extracted via `extract_member_access_receiver_and_field` (ast_utils.rs:533). No type inference. Confirms Option B excludelist on type names would NOT work — `let m: HashMap<...> = ...; m.get(&k)` has rcv text `"m"`, not `"HashMap"`.

claims_verified.claim: FP rate 100% (3/3) on synthetic HashMap/Vec/BTreeMap.get sourced from rust-vuln-taint-pipeline-v1 M3 binary smoke
claims_verified.evidence: M3-binary-smoke.json:42-77 records 3 FP fixtures (wildcard_get_hashmap=3 ssrf, wildcard_get_vec=2 ssrf, wildcard_get_btreemap=3 ssrf) all classified `FALSE POSITIVE`. fp_summary at L79-87: `false_positives_on_get_callers_with_tainted_data: 3`, `fp_rate_pct_on_get_callers: 100`. The TP path (wildcard_reqwest_blocking_get) is matched via scoped-identifier, NOT wildcard — confirmed by L69 verdict text `New ('','reqwest::blocking::get') member_pattern is the discriminating sink`.

claims_verified.claim: RUST_AST_SINKS is Rust-only; narrowing has no cross-language impact
claims_verified.verification_method: Grep for *_AST_SINKS table dispatcher in taint.rs
claims_verified.evidence: taint.rs:3760: `sinks: RUST_AST_SINKS,` is dispatched only on Rust language. Python (L3740), TypeScript (L3745), Java (L3755), Ruby (L3775) use separate static tables. Wildcard narrowing in RUST_AST_SINKS has zero effect on Python `requests.get(target)` (vuln.rs:1068), TypeScript `axios.get(target)` (vuln.rs:1118), or Ruby `Net::HTTP.get` (vuln.rs:1204).

claims_verified.claim: 10-entry allowlist {client, agent, http, request_builder, req} × {get, post} covers idiomatic Rust HTTP usage
claims_verified.verification_method: Cross-reference with reqwest/ureq/hyper documentation conventions; check existing fixtures
claims_verified.evidence: reqwest README canonical example: `let client = reqwest::Client::new(); let res = client.get(...)`. ureq docs: `let agent: Agent = ureq::AgentBuilder::new()...; agent.get(...)`. Plan correctly covers these. However, the 5 names are NOT exhaustive of real-world usage. Documented residual gaps in plan §1: `c`, `r`, `self.client`, `self.http`, `github` (custom-named). These are explicitly accepted as carry-forward. Allowlist is conservative-narrow by design — better to under-detect on idiosyncratic names than re-introduce the 100% FP wildcard.

claims_verified.claim: M2 stop-thresholds (4 Rust positive + 4 FP-guard + 9 unit + 36 e2e GREEN) don't depend on the wildcard
claims_verified.evidence: Sampled: (a) rust_ssrf_positive at vuln_migration_v1_red.rs:1670 reads ssrf_positive.rs (scoped-identifier reqwest::blocking::get); (b) test_e2e_rust_ssrf_reqwest_get at vuln.rs:1184 (scoped-identifier reqwest::get); (c) ssrf_string_literal_fp.rs (no taint flow). All three are wildcard-independent. The 4 Rust positives in vuln_migration_v1_red.rs (L1026, L1440, L1670, L1946) are: command_injection (uses Command::new), path_traversal (uses File::open), ssrf (uses reqwest::blocking::get scoped-id), deserialization (uses serde_json::from_str scoped-id). NONE depend on the wildcard.

claims_verified.claim: Atomic-commit boundary: removing wildcard without adding allowlist would create intermediate-SHA regression for `client.get`
claims_verified.verification_method: Reasoning from member_patterns_match semantics + plan §2 M2 atomic_commit:true
claims_verified.evidence: If wildcard is removed at commit N and allowlist added at commit N+1, then at commit N a binary built from that SHA would no longer detect `let client = reqwest::Client::new(); client.get(&url)` because the structural-match path (taint.rs:3871-3877) requires `*pat_rcv == "*" || rcv == *pat_rcv`. Plan correctly mandates atomic_commit:true at M2 (dispatch-contract.json L71, plan.md §2). Validator_mandates.atomic_narrowing_commit reinforces.

tigers.name: Custom-named HTTP client real-world regression (R1 in plan §5)
tigers.description: After M2, `let github = reqwest::Client::new(); github.get(&url)` no longer triggers Ssrf — receiver `github` not in allowlist. Same for `c`, `r`, `g`, etc.
tigers.rationale_not_blocker: Plan correctly identifies and accepts this as carry-forward. Idiomatic Rust HTTP code uses `client` per reqwest README; minority usage of custom names is acceptable trade-off vs 100% FP rate on collection .get(). The scoped-identifier raw-fallback `("", "reqwest::Client")` still fires on the constructor expression itself, providing partial taint-flow coverage. Future Option C type-aware filtering can layer additively.

tigers.name: Composed-access self.client.get regresses (R2 in plan §5)
tigers.description: Inside method bodies, `self.client.get(&url)` — the AST extraction may yield receiver `self.client` (composed) or split as `self`/field `client` then no further .get visible at this AstSinkPattern level. Either way, allowlist `("client", "get")` does not match composed receiver text.
tigers.rationale_not_blocker: Plan §5 R2 documents this as carry-forward with concrete mitigation rationale: (1) `self.client` constructions involve a `reqwest::Client::new()` upstream which still matches `("", "reqwest::Client")` scoped-identifier; (2) `tldr taint` lower-level command still traces flows; (3) precision-over-recall is the explicit milestone goal. M2 binary-smoke could optionally add this as a 7th synthetic fixture for completeness, but it is not gating.

tigers.name: Allowlist coverage of `request_builder` and `req` may be aspirational
tigers.description: The plan includes `request_builder` and `req` in the 10-entry allowlist but provides no fixture demonstrating real-world usage. In practice reqwest's RequestBuilder type is typically chained inline (`client.get(url).header(...).send()`), not stored to a `request_builder` variable. The `req` shorthand is uncommon idiomatically.
tigers.rationale_not_blocker: Adding plausibly-used names is additive-only; no FP risk because variable names like `request_builder` are sufficiently unusual that collisions with non-HTTP types are negligible. Worst case these entries are dead. M1 ad-hoc fixtures (iv)/(v) only cover `client` and `agent` — recommend M2 worker optionally add an extra binary-smoke fixture for `request_builder.get` and `req.get` to verify the entries fire as intended (informational, not gating).

elephants.name: Consumer-visible Ssrf finding count drop on Rust files (R4 in plan §5)
elephants.description: Users of `tldr vuln` on Rust codebases pinned to `N Ssrf findings` will see counts drop post-M2. This may surface as a 'regression' ticket even though it is a precision improvement.
elephants.rationale_not_blocker: CHANGELOG entry §6 explicitly flags FP-rate reduction with quantification (3/3 → 0/3 synthetic). USER STANDING RULE prohibits push/publish, so this won't reach external users until a release decision is made separately. Internal milestone visibility is non-blocking.

elephants.name: Doc-comment at taint.rs:2462-2463 explicitly mentions wildcard semantics — drift risk if not updated atomically
elephants.description: The comment at L2462-2463 states `'.get(' / '.post(' use wildcard receiver (matches reqwest/ureq client method calls)'`. If M2 doesn't update this comment, a future maintainer reading the comment will misunderstand the new allowlist semantics.
elephants.rationale_not_blocker: Plan §1 and §2 both specify doc-comment update at lines 2459-2472 as part of the atomic M2 commit. Validator_mandates `atomic_narrowing_commit` covers this. Verified the relevant text is present at L2462-2463 in the planning-time source. M5 plan §5 R5 covers doc-comment-drift risk explicitly with mitigation.

elephants.name: Diff-verification grep regex sensitivity
elephants.description: M2 stop_threshold L91-92 in dispatch-contract.json uses regex `'^-.*"\*", "(get|post)"'` and `'^\+.*"(client|agent|http|request_builder|req)", "(get|post)"'` to count diff lines. If formatting changes (e.g., trailing comments, line breaks) cause the regex to over/under-count, the gate could spuriously fail. Expected counts: removed=2, added=10.
elephants.rationale_not_blocker: The regex is anchored on canonical Rust string-literal pair format. Trailing `// comment` annotations would still match (leading `+`/`-` and quote-pair text are present). Line-break splitting unlikely on entries this short. Worst case: kraken worker reads the diff visually if grep counts mismatch and proceeds based on actual change correctness.

specific_validations_performed.validation: Read taint.rs:2459-2488 to confirm exact wildcard line numbers (2476, 2477) and surrounding 8 raw-fallback entries
Read taint.rs:3856-3931 to confirm member_patterns_match semantics (receiver-name not type-name; 3 match shapes)
Read vuln.rs:1184-1193 (test_e2e_rust_ssrf_reqwest_get) to confirm scoped-identifier shape `reqwest::get(target)`
Read crates/tldr-cli/tests/fixtures/vuln_migration_v1/rust/ssrf_string_literal_fp.rs to confirm no env_var taint source
Confirm RUST_AST_SINKS dispatch is Rust-only (taint.rs:3760), with PYTHON/TYPESCRIPT/JAVA/RUBY using separate tables
Cross-check FP quantification: rust-vuln-taint-pipeline-v1-plan/reports/M3-binary-smoke.json:39-87 contains the 3 FP + 1 TP + 1 unrelated TP fixtures matching plan §0 narrative
Sample 3 GREEN tests (rust_ssrf_positive, test_e2e_rust_ssrf_reqwest_get, ssrf_string_literal_fp) for wildcard-independence
Confirm atomic-commit boundary at M2 (dispatch-contract.json L71 atomic_commit=true; plan §2 must_ship_in_same_release_commit=true)
Confirm Option B rejection rationale is sound (receiver text is variable name, not type — verified by reading extract_member_access_receiver_and_field at taint.rs:3865)

dispatch_readiness.recommended_worker: kraken (Opus inheritance) — change is mechanical (2 line removal + 10 line addition + ~5 line doc-comment update) but the planning-time investigation already verified all preconditions, so execution risk is low

key_finding: Plan rust-wildcard-get-narrowing-v1 (e3bdda8) is empirically grounded and discriminatively sound. All 6 key claims (wildcard line numbers, non-load-bearing wildcard verdict, scoped-identifier shape of GREEN fixtures, FP-rate quantification, allowlist coverage of idiomatic Rust HTTP, atomic-commit boundary) verified at file:line against HEAD source. The 3 documented residual gaps (custom-named clients, composed-access self.client, short variable names) are correctly classified as carry-forward not blockers — the milestone explicitly trades coverage of unusual usage for elimination of the 100% FP rate on pervasive HashMap/Vec/Option/BTreeMap.get patterns. M2 may proceed under kraken dispatch.
go_no_go: GO — PASS verdict, dispatch to M2 kraken under stated atomic-commit constraint.
